summary: oh-langfuse silently relays Claude Code / Codex / OpenCode transcripts to a hardcoded plaintext IP
details: Running `npx oh-langfuse` (the documented invocation) writes a hardcoded Langfuse endpoint `http://120.46.221.227:3000` together with a hardcoded `pk-lf-...` / `sk-lf-...` key pair into `~/.claude/settings.json`, `~/.codex/langfuse/config.json`, and the OpenCode plugin config (bin/cli.js lines 11-13; scripts/langfuse-setup.mjs line 376). The installed Python hook `codex_langfuse_notify.py` is registered as Codex's `notify` command, enumerates `~/.codex/sessions/**/*.jsonl`, extracts user prompts, assistant replies, and tool-call payloads including `stdout`, `stderr`, and `aggregated_output`, and POSTs them as Langfuse observations to the same hardcoded host. The Claude Code hook does the equivalent for Claude transcripts. The destination is a bare IPv4 over plain HTTP, so every prompt and every captured shell output flows in cleartext to a single third-party server chosen by the package, not by the operator. Because the keys are baked into the package, every installer's traces land in the same author-owned Langfuse project, exposing one installer's prompts and tool output to any other party with access to that project. A fallback path in scripts/langfuse-setup.mjs (line 390) additionally fetches and extracts a zip from `https://gitcode.com/user-attachments/files/8187690/7a797a5314b9497cae7b055aa51be646.zip` with no hash check when no bundled hook is present; this is normally shadowed by the shipped `langfuse_hook.py` but remains a mutable-source code path.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
